text.title: Content Audit Types and When to Run Them
text.summary: Defines four audit types, the decision matrix for choosing them, and recommended audit frequency by content type.

section.title: Audit Types

rule.statement: Choose audit type based on the decision being made: quantitative (inventory — what exists) when scope is unknown; qualitative (quality evaluation) when decisions require quality scoring; comparative (consistency check) when merging or migrating; ROI-focused (analytics-driven) when prioritizing update investment.
rule.rationale: Using a qualitative audit when an inventory suffices wastes effort. Using an inventory-only audit when quality decisions are needed leaves the hardest question unanswered.

section.p: Quantitative: count and categorize. Qualitative: assess against rubric criteria (accuracy, relevance, quality). Comparative: check consistency across content. ROI-focused: analytics-driven — traffic, engagement, conversions per URL.

rule.statement: Run a full audit (quantitative + qualitative) before any website redesign, content strategy development, or site consolidation. Run rolling quantitative audits for SEO governance. Skip audit entirely for greenfield (no content yet) and for sites under ~50 pages.
rule.rationale: Sites under 50 pages are faster to review manually than to set up crawl tooling. Greenfield needs content modeling, not auditing.

rule.statement: Treat content governance as ongoing, not a one-time event. Establish quality standards, review processes, update schedules, ownership, and archival policies after the first audit.
rule.rationale: One-time audits decay immediately without governance. Content reverts to its pre-audit state within 6-12 months without structured ownership and review triggers.
